rationale: The narrator hires Bartleby as a scrivener and pays him for copying.
The narrator repeatedly calls Bartleby his friend and tries to aid him.
The narrator dismisses Bartleby, moves offices, and tries to make him leave.
Turkey is one of the narrator’s copyists and performs legal copying work.
The narrator remonstrates with Turkey and tries to reduce his afternoon labor.
Nippers is the narrator’s second copyist and writes legal documents for him.
Nippers causes the narrator annoyance through irritability and professional overreach.
Ginger Nut works as office boy, errand runner, cleaner, and student at law.
Ginger Nut’s father sends him to the office to improve his prospects.
Ginger Nut’s father arranges his son’s placement in the narrator’s office.
Turkey and Nippers work together as copyists in the narrator’s office.
They mutually confer nicknames and dine together on at least one occasion.
Ginger Nut serves Turkey by fetching cakes and apples during office work.
Ginger Nut serves Nippers by fetching cakes and apples during office work.
Bartleby joins Turkey as a fellow copyist in the narrator’s office.
Turkey threatens to black Bartleby’s eyes over his refusals.
Bartleby and Nippers are both scriveners in the same office.
Nippers says he would kick Bartleby out and curses his stubbornness.
Ginger Nut buys and delivers ginger-nuts to Bartleby for a fee.
The narrator worked professionally for John Jacob Astor and values his opinion.
The woman sweeps, dusts, scrubs the office, and keeps one of its keys.
Both have office-related keyholding or maintenance roles around the narrator’s chambers.
Nippers receives ambiguous visitors whom he calls clients.
One supposed client appears to be a dun presenting a bill.
Ginger Nut frequently buys cakes and apples from stalls near the Post Office and Custom House.
The narrator’s legal acquaintances visit his chambers and comment on Bartleby.
Their remarks about Bartleby pressure the narrator to remove him.
The attorney seeks information from Bartleby about the narrator’s whereabouts.
Bartleby refuses to answer the attorney’s professional inquiry.
During a Reference, the lawyer asks Bartleby to fetch papers from his office.
Bartleby declines the lawyer’s request, causing the lawyer to stare and complain.
They gather in the narrator’s room during a legal Reference.
Their presence makes Bartleby’s idleness a professional embarrassment.
The new lawyer confronts the narrator about Bartleby after occupying his former rooms.
The new tenant expects Bartleby to copy after taking over the office.
The new lawyer turns Bartleby out and demands the narrator remove him.
The landlord holds the narrator responsible as Bartleby’s last known employer.
The landlord pressures the narrator to remove Bartleby at once.
The landlord sends for police and has Bartleby removed as a vagrant.
The landlord intervenes after the new lawyer cannot make Bartleby leave.
The landlord acts on complaints from tenants disturbed by Bartleby.
The tenants cannot stand Bartleby haunting the stairs and entry.
The tenants demand that the narrator take Bartleby away.
Constables remove Bartleby to the Tombs as a vagrant.
The police act after the landlord sends for them.
Both participate in the institutional process that confines Bartleby.
The officer confirms Bartleby is in the Tombs and permits him limited freedom.
The narrator states his purpose to the Tombs officer and requests an interview.
Turnkeys monitor Bartleby in the prison and report where he has gone.
Turnkeys answer the narrator’s questions and direct him to Bartleby.
The grub-man is hired to provide Bartleby better food in prison.
The narrator pays the grub-man to give Bartleby particular attention.
The turnkey confirms the grub-man’s role in providing food to prisoners.
The grub-man recalls Monroe Edwards from prison and asks whether the narrator knew him.
Rumor says Bartleby had been a subordinate clerk in the Dead Letter Office.
The narrator imagines dead-letter work worsening Bartleby’s hopelessness.
A change in administration reportedly removed Bartleby from his clerkship.
The administration controls appointments or removals at the Dead Letter Office.
